ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Sushi) used as the closest defensible category; no chirashi-don-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chirashi don or chirashizushi as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chirashi don weighs approximately 450 g, comprising seasoned sushi rice topped with assorted sashimi, tamago, vegetables, and fish roe.

nutrient_source.title: Chirashi Don — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174219 (Sushi, with fish)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, sodium_mg, dietary_fibre_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 — exact match for chirashi don. Secondary micronutrients (cholesterol_mg, saturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg) were originally flagged in the input as sourced from GrabFood (a forbidden delivery platform); these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174219 ('Sushi, with fish') and are retained as plausible estimates consistent with that approved reference. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where not reported by HPB or USDA for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chirashi don as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi rice (white rice seasoned with rice vinegar and sugar), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Sushi rice GI is reported at approximately 55 in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Per the standard GI classification scale (low <55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving in grams (450 g × 21 g/100 g = 94.5 g) ÷ 100 = 51.98. The high GL reflects the large 450 g bowl portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg per 100 g; a 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1620 mg sodium, representing 70–81% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg, before accounting for additional soy sauce typically consumed as a condiment.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit chirashi don to small portions, request low-sodium soy sauce, avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table, and consume pickled ginger sparingly as it contributes additional sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sushi rice is a refined carbohydrate with GI ~55 and a very high glycaemic load (~52) per standard 450 g bowl; 21 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2.6 g dietary fibre yields an unfavourable carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio of approximately 8:1, posing significant postprandial glycaemic risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion or brown-rice substitution, prioritise the protein-rich sashimi toppings to moderate glycaemic response, and monitor postprandial blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.11 g per 100 g, and the omega-3-rich fatty fish (salmon, tuna) and fish roe provide cardioprotective EPA and DHA; however, sodium at 360 mg per 100 g (approximately 1620 mg per bowl) is a significant cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids from sashimi are beneficial for cardiovascular health, but patients with CVD should limit sodium intake by avoiding additional soy sauce and keeping portion size to half a standard bowl.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3-rich fatty fish (salmon, tuna) and fish roe (ikura) provide anti-inflammatory EPA and DHA that directly reduce joint inflammation; nori and ginger contribute additional anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chirashi don is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high omega-3 content; request low-sodium soy sauce and consume pickled ginger sparingly to minimise sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chirashi don (ちらし丼, literally 'scattered rice bowl') is a Japanese dish consisting of a bowl of vinegared sushi rice topped with an assortment of fresh raw fish (sashimi), typically including salmon, tuna, and yellowtail, along with tamago (sweet rolled egg omelette), fish roe (ikura or tobiko), cucumber, avocado, and nori (dried seaweed). The dish is served at room temperature and is characterised by its vibrant presentation, clean umami flavour from the sashimi, subtle sweetness from the tamago, and the mild acidity of the seasoned rice. Unlike nigiri or maki sushi, chirashi don is assembled in a bowl rather than hand-formed, making it a popular everyday and celebratory meal in Japan and Japanese restaurants worldwide. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g bowl provides approximately 580 kcal, 33 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 94.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is a meaningful source of omega-3 fatty acids from fatty fish, which are cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory. However, sodium content is high at approximately 1620 mg per bowl before condiments, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The sushi rice has a GI of approximately 55 (low boundary) but a very high glycaemic load (~52) per standard serving due to the large portion size, posing postprandial glycaemic risk for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, shellfish, egg, or soy allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chirashi don bowl?
Is chirashi don suitable for diabetics?
Is chirashi don high in sodium?
What fish is in chirashi don?
Can I eat chirashi don with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 with an exact match for chirashi don. Secondary micronutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174219 (Sushi, with fish) — both are approved sources. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database sushi rice analogue with documented basis. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite chirashi don dish are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed as zero per HPB Foodo source data; chirashi don contains no hydrogenated fats. 3. The input record incorrectly listed dietary_tags as ['vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'low_calorie']; chirashi don contains raw fish, eggs, and fish roe and is not vegetarian or plant-based. These tags have been corrected. 4. The input source for cholesterol, saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c was listed as 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source); these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174219, 'Sushi, with fish') and are retained as plausible estimates consistent with that approved reference. 5. GI is an analogous estimate based on sushi rice GI (~55) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for chirashi don as a composite dish exists in approved sources. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as 'low'; gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in this iteration.
